hypothesis: We believe mid-market advertisers (50-500 employees) waste significant time manually adapting creative assets across channels (Meta, Google, TikTok, programmatic) because they lack automated tools that provide real-time performance feedback at the creative level, leading to suboptimal ad performance and inefficient use of media budgets.

assumptions.statement: Mid-market advertisers manage creative across 3+ channels simultaneously
Manual creative adaptation is a top-3 workflow pain point for these teams
Existing tools (Canva, Adobe, platform-native) don't solve cross-channel creative optimisation
Advertisers can interpret creative-level performance data without dedicated analyst support
Real-time cross-channel creative performance data is technically accessible via platform APIs
Creative teams would trust AI-generated optimisation recommendations enough to act on them
Advertisers would pay $500-2000/mo for this capability
This tool doesn't cannibalise existing analytics products already in their stack
Creative performance attribution across channels is accurate enough to be actionable
Legal/brand compliance review processes don't block automated creative changes

scripts.raw_text:
1. Don't you find it frustrating to manage creative assets across multiple channels?
2. Would you use a tool that automatically optimised your creatives?
3. How helpful would AI-powered creative recommendations be for your team?
4. Tell me about the last time you launched a campaign across multiple channels.
5. Walk me through how you currently decide which creative to use on which channel.
6. If we built a dashboard that showed real-time creative performance, would you pay for it?
7. Is creative optimisation a problem for your team?
8. What tools do you currently use for creative management, and how do you measure their effectiveness?
9. Describe the last time a creative asset underperformed. What happened?
10. How much time per week does your team spend on creative adaptation?

sessions.raw_notes:
P1 â€” Marketing Director, DTC skincare brand, 85 employees

Runs campaigns on Meta, Google Shopping, TikTok, and programmatic via The Trade Desk. Team of 3 manages creative across all channels.

Described last campaign launch (Q4 holiday push): Created 12 hero images and 6 video variants in Canva. Spent 'probably two full days' manually resizing and reformatting for each platform's specs. 'It's the most tedious part of my week, honestly.' Used a spreadsheet to track which creative ran where.

No cross-channel performance view. Checks each platform's native dashboard separately. 'By the time I've looked at Meta, checked Google, opened TTD... the day's gone.' Estimates 8-10 hours per week just on creative management across channels.

When asked about underperformance: A Meta carousel 'tanked' but the same imagery performed well on Google Shopping. Took 4 days to notice because Meta reporting lag and manual checking. 'If I'd caught it day one, I could've shifted budget. Instead we burned through about $3K on a dud.'

Currently pays for Canva Pro ($30/mo team), plus each platform's built-in tools. Would 'definitely consider' a dedicated tool but concerned about adding 'yet another dashboard.' Mentioned budget approval would need to come from CFO for anything above $500/mo.

P2 â€” Media Buyer, performance marketing agency, handles 8 client accounts

Skeptical from the start. 'We've seen a dozen tools promise cross-channel creative insights. Most of them can barely pull accurate data from one platform, let alone four.'

Uses platform-native tools exclusively. 'I trust Meta's reporting for Meta, Google's for Google. The moment you aggregate, you introduce attribution errors.' Cited a specific example: a client tried a cross-channel tool last year that double-counted conversions and led to wrong budget allocation.

Doesn't see creative adaptation as a major pain point for HIS workflow. 'My job is buying, not making creatives. The creative team handles production. I just tell them what's working and what's not.' The handoff between media buying and creative production is where things slow down â€” 'I flag a winner on Slack, creative team takes 2-3 days to make variants.'

When pressed on whether a tool could speed up that handoff: 'Maybe. But you'd need buy-in from the creative team too, and they're protective of their process. They don't want an algorithm telling them what looks good.'

On pricing: 'Agency margins are thin. We're not paying $2K/mo per client for something we're not sure works. Maybe $200-300/mo if it genuinely saved us an hour a day per account. But prove it first.'

On AI recommendations: 'I've tested AI creative tools. The suggestions are generic â€” increase contrast, use brighter colors, add urgency. Nothing I couldn't tell a junior in five minutes.'

P3 â€” Creative Operations Lead, e-commerce fashion retailer, 200 employees

Manages production of 200+ creative assets per month across Meta, Google, Pinterest, TikTok, and programmatic. Team of 5 designers plus 2 production coordinators.

Has built extensive internal workarounds: a Notion database tracking every asset, which channels it's been deployed to, and performance notes pulled manually each Monday. 'It's held together with duct tape and good intentions.' The Monday performance review takes one person's full morning â€” 4 hours of pulling data from each platform and updating the tracker.

Described a workflow gap: designs are created in Figma, exported, then manually uploaded to each platform with platform-specific adjustments (aspect ratios, text overlay rules, safe zones). 'We lose at least a day per campaign just on the mechanical reformatting. The creative thinking takes an afternoon â€” the logistics take a week.'

When asked about existing tools: tried Celtra for automated creative production 18 months ago. 'The templating worked but the performance insights were garbage. It couldn't tell us anything we didn't already know from the platform dashboards.' Cancelled after 6 months at $1,800/mo.

Would pay for a tool that genuinely connected performance data to creative decisions: 'If you could show me that this color palette outperforms that one on TikTok but not on Meta â€” with real data, not AI hallucinations â€” I'd pay $1,500/mo tomorrow.' But needs to see proof: 'Demo it with our actual data for a month. If it works, we'll buy it.'

On AI trust: 'I trust AI for production tasks â€” resizing, reformatting, generating variants from a template. I do NOT trust AI for creative judgment. Don't tell my designers an algorithm thinks their work is bad.'

P4 â€” Growth Marketer, B2B SaaS company, 120 employees

Minimal creative resources. No dedicated designer â€” uses Canva templates and occasionally contracts a freelance designer for bigger campaigns. Runs ads primarily on LinkedIn, Google Search, and some Meta retargeting.

Creative adaptation isn't a major pain point because creative variety is low: 'Honestly, we run maybe 5-6 ad variants at a time. I can manage that in a spreadsheet. My bigger problem is knowing which MESSAGE resonates, not which IMAGE performs.'

Doesn't currently track creative performance at the asset level. Looks at campaign-level metrics (CAC, pipeline generated, demo requests) and attributes roughly. 'Creative is one variable among many â€” the landing page, the audience, the bidding strategy. I can't isolate creative impact and I'm not sure anyone can reliably.'

When asked about cross-channel: 'LinkedIn and Google are such different beasts that the same creative never runs on both. We design specifically for each. So cross-channel optimisation doesn't really apply to us.'

On pricing: 'For our stage and ad spend (~$30K/mo), I can't justify a creative tool above $300/mo. And it would need to help with messaging, not just visual optimisation.'

Interesting signal: mentioned that the SALES team has strong opinions on which ad creative drives better leads. 'Our AEs tell me certain ad styles bring in tyre-kickers vs. serious buyers. But I have no way to connect that feedback loop to the creative production.'

P5 â€” Brand Manager, consumer packaged goods company, 350 employees

Primary concern is brand consistency, not performance optimisation. 'Every time someone suggests automating creative, I break out in hives. We spent two years building brand guidelines that are finally consistent. An AI that starts changing headlines and colors to optimise clicks could undo all of that.'

Described a past incident: a performance marketing team at a previous company used dynamic creative optimisation (DCO) on Meta. 'It started A/B testing headline variants that were technically higher-performing but completely off-brand. One ad said something like BUY NOW MEGA SALE which... we're a premium brand. That can't happen.'

When asked about the time spent on creative management: 'We have a process and it works. Yes, reformatting takes time, but our agency handles the production. My concern is the QUALITY and CONSISTENCY of what goes out, not the speed.'

On cross-channel performance tracking: does work with a media agency that provides monthly cross-channel reports. 'I get a deck every month. It's always 3 weeks late and tells me things I can't act on anymore. But at least it's auditable and I can show it to my VP.'

Would consider a tool IF it had strong brand guardrails: 'If you could guarantee that no creative goes live without matching our brand guidelines â€” color codes, font usage, tone of voice, exclusion zones â€” then maybe. But the moment the AI starts freelancing, I'm out.'

On willingness to pay: 'Our agency fees already cover creative production and reporting. I'm not adding another vendor unless it replaces something. What would this replace?'

Interesting friction: revealed that brand compliance review adds 2-3 days to every campaign launch. 'Every asset gets reviewed by me and our legal team. If your tool could pre-check compliance, THAT would save time. The creative optimisation bit I could take or leave.'

P6 — Senior Director of Digital Marketing, Fortune-500 CPG company, 12,000 employees

Manages a $40M digital media budget across 18 brands globally. Has a dedicated in-house creative studio of 22 people, a DAM system (Bynder), and agency relationships with three holding company shops.

"We have every tool in the world and we still have the same problem — getting the right creative spec into the right placement on time. Bynder has 50,000 assets in it and half the time the media team can't find the approved version, let alone the right size."

Their creative workflow has six approval stages. By the time creative is approved, the campaign has often already launched with the previous cycle's assets. She described a situation last quarter where they ran a Christmas campaign in January on YouTube because the approved creative didn't arrive in time and the team didn't want to pause spend.

"The enterprise tools solve for storage and approval governance. They don't solve for format adaptation. That's still a person with Photoshop."

Her team has a full-time role — "Creative Trafficker" — whose entire job is resizing and adapting approved creative to platform specs. That role costs approximately $85,000 fully loaded per year. She's asked twice to automate it; both times IT security blocked the API integrations required with the agency systems.

"I'd pay for a tool that just did the resizing automatically. But it has to integrate with Bynder, and it has to fit inside our vendor approval process, which takes six months minimum. That's the blocker."

She's skeptical that a startup can navigate enterprise procurement. Her last three pilot evaluations — all promising tools — died at the security review stage. "We're interested in the problem space, but we're probably not your customer until you have an enterprise security certification and an existing integration with Bynder."

Confirming on pain severity. Skeptical on addressability for enterprise segment.
sessions.summary: Senior Director at Fortune-500 CPG with a $40M budget and a dedicated creative trafficker role costing ~$85K/year purely for resizing and adapting creative. The pain is real and measurable, but enterprise procurement cycles (6+ months, security review requirements, DAM integration mandates) make them unlikely early adopters. Key insight: confirms pain but challenges the market entry strategy — the mid-market may be the right beachhead before targeting enterprise.

sessions.raw_notes:
P7 — Head of Paid Social, DTC fitness brand, 200 employees. Recently churned from Celtra (dynamic creative platform) after 14 months.

Left Celtra three months ago after the platform failed to deliver on its promise of automated creative adaptation. The tool required a full HTML5 template rebuild from their agency — a $45,000 project — before they could unlock the automation features. By the time the templates were done, their creative direction had changed and the templates were obsolete.

"Celtra promised the world. We spent $45K on the setup and then another $120K on the license before we decided it was never going to work for us. The product wasn't bad — the implementation model was broken."

Now running entirely manual process. Three freelancers on Upwork who turn around creative resizes at approximately 6-8 hour turnaround. Cost is about $800/month.

"It's not scalable but it's predictable. I know what I'm paying and I know what I'm getting."

His core frustration is with tools that oversell the ease of setup. "Every vendor in this space tells you it's plug-and-play. Then you get into it and there's a six-month implementation. I've been burned twice now. I'd need to see a tool that works in under an hour of setup before I'd even consider piloting it."

Uses a Notion database to track which creative sizes exist for each campaign. Would pay for the right solution — budget of $2-3K/month — but has a very high bar for 'works out of the box' given prior experience. Wants a self-serve trial before any sales conversation.

Strongly confirming on pain. Strongly challenging on ease-of-adoption claims.
sessions.summary: Head of Paid Social who churned from a $165K enterprise creative platform investment after it failed to deliver automation without a complex implementation. Now using Upwork freelancers for $800/month — functional but unscalable. Provides the clearest articulation of the implementation-complexity failure mode: tools that promise automation but require months of setup before delivering value. Will not engage without a self-serve trial and near-zero setup time.

sessions.raw_notes:
P8 — VP Finance, mid-market e-commerce retailer, 150 employees. Does not manage creative workflows directly but approves all marketing technology purchases over $5,000.

Agreed to the interview to understand 'what the marketing team keeps asking me to buy.' Has approved three martech tools in the past 18 months and been burned twice — tools that were purchased, underused, and cancelled within 12 months.

"I look at two things: what is the hard-dollar saving, and what is the implementation risk. Marketing always comes to me with the feature demo. I need someone to model out what it replaces and what it costs to implement."

When asked about the creative resizing workflow, he pulled up a spreadsheet he'd built to track tool ROI. He calculates the marketing team spends approximately 12 hours per week on creative adaptation tasks across three people. At their fully loaded hourly rate of $65, that's $40,560 annually in labor. The tools they've been asked to buy typically cost $24,000-$60,000/year.

"The math usually works, but then implementation adds 20-30% on top, and then the team still ends up doing it manually anyway because the tool doesn't quite fit the workflow. So I'm paying for the tool AND the manual labor."

He would approve a tool if: (1) it has a clear payback period under 12 months, (2) implementation cost is capped and contractually guaranteed, and (3) there's a 90-day out clause.

"I'm not anti-investment. I'm anti-commitment before proof."

Neutral on the specific pain (defers to marketing team). Challenging on ROI model and commitment structure.
sessions.summary: VP Finance who controls the martech budget and evaluates purchases through a strict ROI lens — he calculates $40K/year in creative adaptation labor cost but has been burned by tools that didn't reduce the labor. Provides the procurement decision framework: payback under 12 months, capped implementation cost, 90-day out clause, and a paid pilot before annual commitment. Critical insight: the finance buyer cares about what the tool replaces, not what it enables.

sessions.raw_notes:
P9 — Digital Media Manager, mid-market fashion retailer, based in Amsterdam, 300 employees across EU and UK.

Runs campaigns across Meta, Google, TikTok, Snapchat, and a Netherlands-specific platform (Ster). Managing creative across 6 markets in 4 languages (Dutch, German, French, English). GDPR compliance affects creative personalization — cannot use retargeting pixels that many creative optimization tools depend on.

"Our biggest creative challenge is not the resize — it's the localization. We have 40 active creative variants per campaign across markets and languages. A resize tool helps maybe 20% of the problem."

The localization workflow involves a translation agency, an internal country manager review, and legal sign-off for financial services language. Each creative cycle takes 12-14 days minimum.

"By the time everything is approved across markets, the campaign opportunity has often passed."

Meta's Advantage+ campaign format has partially helped — she now uses it for prospecting in each market separately, which removes some creative complexity. But it doesn't help with TikTok, Snapchat, or Ster.

Her primary pain is coordination overhead, not technical execution. "We have the tools — Canva for quick edits, the agency for production. What we don't have is a single place to see which creative is approved for which market and channel. We're running that in a shared Google Sheet."

GDPR impact: most 'smart' creative optimization tools require pixel-level data she cannot legally collect for certain user segments. "Tools built for the US market rarely account for consent management complexity. That rules out probably 60% of the vendors we look at."

Confirming on complexity/coordination pain. Challenging on most US-built tools being fit for EU market.
sessions.summary: EU-based media manager running campaigns across 6 markets in 4 languages, where the creative challenge is localization complexity and GDPR consent management rather than simple resizing. US-built tools that depend on retargeting pixels fail EU compliance requirements, ruling out ~60% of vendors she evaluates. Key insight: the international market has a materially different problem definition — localization and consent management are the core issues, not format adaptation.

sessions.raw_notes:
P10 — Performance Marketing Lead, SaaS company, 180 employees. Has built a semi-automated creative production pipeline using Zapier, Google Sheets, and Bannerbear API.

Identified the creative resizing problem three years ago when he was spending 8-10 hours per week on it personally. Spent two months building an automation: brief enters a Google Form, triggers a Zapier workflow, which calls Bannerbear API to generate standard banner sizes, then uploads to a shared Dropbox folder with Slack notification.

"It's not pretty but it works. Reduces my time from 8 hours to maybe 45 minutes per campaign — the 45 minutes is QA and any custom sizes Bannerbear can't handle."

Current limitations: Bannerbear handles static images but not video. Video still requires manual export from After Effects with 4-5 different size presets.

"Video is about 40% of our creative budget now, so my automation only solves 60% of the problem."

Has looked at all the major tools in this space. Has tried Storyteq (too expensive, £3K/month), Madgicx (focused on targeting, not creative), and Celtra (enterprise implementation burden).

"I've done the research. Nothing hits the sweet spot of: works for video, affordable for a 180-person company, doesn't require an agency to set up."

"The tool I want doesn't exist yet. It's Bannerbear but with native video support and some basic AI for copy localization. I'd pay $800-1,500/month for that."

Is actively evaluating two new entrants in the space. Would switch from his custom solution if the product hit his criteria — and he has the technical chops to evaluate it properly within one sprint.

Strongly confirming on pain. Skeptical because he's tried everything and built his own solution. Provides the clearest product spec of any participant.
sessions.summary: Power user who built his own creative automation pipeline (Zapier + Bannerbear) that saves ~7 hours/week but only covers static image formats — video, animated GIFs, and HTML5 units still require manual work. Has evaluated every tool in the market and found none that solve video + affordability + easy setup. Provides the most precise product spec: video support, sub-$1,500/month, self-serve setup. His criteria and his current workaround define the MVP feature bar better than any other participant.
